title: AWS Access-Key Compromise and S3 Data Exfiltration
description: Response playbook for a leaked or stolen AWS IAM access key used to enumerate the account and exfiltrate data from S3. Triggered by a public-key-exposure alert (e.g. a key committed to a repo), a GuardDuty finding, or an anomalous CloudTrail spike in S3 read/list activity.
author: Cognis Digital

steps.name: Ensure CloudTrail and detective controls are on
steps.description: Confirm CloudTrail (management and S3 data events) is enabled org-wide and immutable, and that GuardDuty and key-exposure scanning are active.

steps.name: Pre-authorize key disablement
steps.description: Document who may disable IAM keys and detach policies under incident conditions, and keep a break-glass admin path that does not depend on the suspect credential.

steps.name: Confirm the credential is in hostile use
steps.description: Verify the access key is being used from unexpected source IPs/regions and identify the IAM principal it belongs to.
steps.detection: CloudTrail events for the access key ID from new source IPs or user agents; correlate against the exposure alert timestamp.

steps.name: Scope enumeration and data access
steps.description: Determine what the actor listed and read to bound the exposure.
steps.detection: CloudTrail/S3 data events for ListBuckets, GetObject volume spikes, and any CopyObject to an external or attacker-owned bucket.

steps.name: Disable the compromised key immediately
steps.response: Set the access key to Inactive (do not delete yet, to preserve attribution), and attach an explicit deny policy to the principal if it is a role.

steps.name: Block exfiltration paths
steps.response: Tighten bucket policies and block public/cross-account access on affected buckets; revoke any presigned URLs and quarantine suspicious destination buckets.

steps.name: Remove actor persistence in IAM
steps.response: Audit for IAM users, keys, roles, or policy attachments the actor created, remove them, and rotate any other secrets the principal could reach.

steps.name: Reissue credentials and restore least privilege
steps.response: Issue new, least-privilege credentials for the legitimate workload, prefer short-lived role credentials over long-lived keys, and confirm services recover.

steps.name: Close the exposure class
steps.description: Trace how the key leaked (repo, log, CI variable), add secret scanning to that path, and move the workload to short-lived credentials to prevent recurrence.
